commands.review_summary:
This PR persists and broadcasts failed WebChat/Gateway session lifecycle state after dispatch/provider failures, tracks lifecycle run IDs and legacy session keys, updates active-run projection, and adds gateway/UI regression coverage.

Reproducibility: yes. from source inspection rather than an executed repro: current main can clear the WebChat active run after dispatch rejection while leaving the persisted session lifecycle row running. The PR adds regression tests for that gateway path and related stale terminal patch races.
commands.review_followup: No repair lane is needed; this protected maintainer PR has no actionable review findings and needs normal maintainer/CI validation.

target.title: Fix WebChat dispatch failure session status
target.body:
## Summary

- Persist a terminal failed session lifecycle event when WebChat dispatch rejects before an assistant reply.
- Preserve terminal lifecycle errors from embedded-agent/provider failures when model fallback is exhausted, so fallback assistant text no longer clears the pending failure before the grace timer finalizes the session.
- Broadcast the matching `sessions.changed` payload with `status: "failed"` and accurate `hasActiveRun` so Control UI can clear the active run state.
- Add regression tests for pre-agent dispatch rejection, aborted dispatch rejection, embedded lifecycle-error finalization, exhausted fallback handling, and legacy session-key persistence.

## Root Cause

The WebChat `chat.send` async dispatch error path cleaned up the active run and broadcast a chat error, but it did not update persisted session lifecycle state. That left `sessions.list` able to return `status: "running"` with `hasActiveRun: false`, which kept the Control UI in a permanent running state.

For the real embedded-agent provider rejection path, the embedded run emitted `lifecycle:error`, but a later non-terminal fallback/assistant event cleared the pending terminal lifecycle error before the grace timer finalized it. The session therefore stayed persisted as `running` even though the active run had ended.

## Validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.list-changed.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/gateway/server-chat.ts src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.ts src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: WebChat sessions no longer remain `status: "running"` with no active run after an embedded AIGW provider 400 before reply.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw Gateway from this PR worktree using `atlassian-aigw/openclaw-localtest-missing-model-84352`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: started `openclaw-local-test --repo /Users/jmerhi/repos/claw-working-space/openclaw-pr-84352-review --state-dir ~/.openclaw-local-test-pr84352-provider-400-final --model openclaw-localtest-missing-model-84352 --no-channels --ttl 2h`, sent a `chat.send` WebSocket request to the local Gateway, then waited for the lifecycle grace window.
- Evidence after fix: local logs showed `400 "Bad Request"`, `LLM request failed: provider rejected the request schema or tool payload.`, and `Embedded agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: provider rejected the request schema or tool payload.`
- Observed result after fix: `~/.openclaw-local-test-pr84352-provider-400-final/agents/super/sessions/sessions.json` showed the affected session with `status: "failed"`, non-null `endedAt`, and non-null `runtimeMs`.
- What was not tested: Slack/Discord channel delivery; local proof used WebChat/Gateway only with channels disabled.


- Video evidence: `/Users/jmerhi/Downloads/Fixing UI Run Status Check Timing.mp4` captures the fixed WebChat run-status behavior locally. This is local evidence and is not committed.
target.author: jesse-merhi
